sentences.text: My name is Jack Lewin or Levin.
The discrepancy is in the pronunciation.
I was born in Lodz, Poland.
And in the Polish language and the Polish alphabet, there is no V.
There is a W, which is pronounced V.
So, the-- originally, the name is Jack Levin, but I go now by the name of Jack Lewin because this is the way it's pronounced here.
I picked this date for the interview, January 27, 1995, because today is my 50th anniversary of my liberation from Auschwitz.
So I thought this would be very appropriate to spend the day to talk about my life, about everything that I went through during the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was born in Lodz, Poland.
From my childhood, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember very much.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are a few things that-- happy moments that come to mind.
Like, for example, I remember I was very young, we used to go for vacations [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a village outside of Lodz not very far.
And a few very funny happenings stick in my mind, because my parents used to talk about them, used to remind me.
I think I was three or four years old.
We were in a place called Andrespol.
My father used to come out for the weekends.
He stayed in town to work.
He was a house painter.
And I was there with my mother.
Apparently, I did something wrong.
I don't remember why, but I deserved some punishment.
We had a little cellar in the room where we stayed for the vacation, so my parents put me in that cellar.
And of course, they kept an eye on me.
At one moment-- at that time I think I was three or three and a half-- they looked into the cellar.
And they saw I was sitting-- we kept some bottles of wine-- I was sitting there with a bottle, sipping the wine.
This is what I remember.
My parents told the story over and over again at every family gathering.
I went to a kindergarten.
What, what year were you born?
I was born in 1927, April 13, 1927.
So this had to have been sometimes '31 or '30.
I think in 1931, at the end of '32, I started going to a kindergarten.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also, very happy memories.
Not many, because I don't remember.
I remember once, I think I was four years old, four and a half.
We had a little house that we played-- oh, it was maybe two or three feet high.
And I jumped off it.
I don't know whether this is the reason or this is-- I was in terrible pain.
They took me home.
They had to take me to the hospital.
And they had to operate on me.
I had a hernia operation at four or four and a half.
I started school, elementary school, in 1933.
The Medem Shul in Lodz was private Yiddish school, where the main language was Yiddish.
And of course, we learned Polish also.
Very good school, very good school, very high standard of learning.
We did a lot of reading.
And also, the school was part of the Yiddish Shul of [NON-ENGLISH] which is called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was an educational-- it was an organization all over Poland, where they started right after first World War, Yiddish co-educational schools for boys and girls.

Also, at, in the fifth grade, it was not officially, but practically officially, automatic that we joined an organization, a children's organization, that was affiliated with the Bund, with the Yiddish Socialist Party, the labor party of, in Poland.
The organization was called SKIF.
These initials, S-K-I-F, Sotsyalistishe Kinder Farband, which is the Socialist Yiddish Children's Organization.
We went to camps.
We had groups, you know, like, seminars every week.
One of the happiest moments of my life before the war-- I remember that it was shortly before the Second World War.
This was either July or August, 1939.
And my parents finally permitted me to go to a summer camp.
It was a four-week camp about 60 or 70 miles from Lodz.
I remember going by bus, and I was sick like a dog on the way.
The camp was in a little village called Zondlowice, which was about 12 kilometers from a town Spala where the Polish president had a summer residence.
In fact, we had an excursion there one day to go there.
And this is very memorable.
These four weeks in that camp, something that I still remember, even though it's already, what, 55 years.
They were the happiest moments in my life before the war.
[INAUDIBLE].
On that camp, it was called the Ninth Socialist Children's Republic in Zondlowice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think there were about 100 children, ages from 12, maybe even younger, 11, till about 15 or 16.
We had a wonderful time.
There were games every day and discussion groups and excursions.
There was one day that we worked in the fields where the peasants-- with the Polish peasants.
It was in July or August one day.
So we worked the whole day in the big heat out in the, in the fields.
These things start coming back when I think about it.
I haven't thought about it for a long time.
And there was a lot of fun when we had the evenings.
We made a radio.
And the older kids, of course, wrote [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] funny stories about the other kids, you know, mixed with politics.
Just like now, we were teasing each other, and of course, the campfires and the singing and the dancing and the camaraderie among the kids and the fights among the kids.
It was a wonderful, wonderful time, something that I carried with me for years in my memory.
I was so happy that my parents permitted me to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Talking about my parents-- my father's name was-- his birth name was Hersch Lipman Levin.
Hershel Levin we called him.
He was a house painter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't really remember very much of his character.
I remember he was a strict father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember once he had a big job with his partner.
They took on-- in a movie theater to paint the chairs, which was very lucky because we were far from being rich, and this was a good prospect to make some money.
But I don't know what happened.
I remember that my father cut himself, his thumb.
The reason I remember because afterwards, years after, I remember when I touched his thumb, here, it was very soft.
He cut his thumb.
Of course, we didn't have any antibiotics or anything.
Apparently, he didn't clean it properly or whatever.
It infected.
And he could not work.
And the job was lost.
I remember that was a big blow for our family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was a housewife and a seamstress.
She used to work for ladies and children.
I remember she used to make children's suits, blouses, all kinds of things.
I remember her sewing.
There's one sound left in my head.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When she put that thimble on and she was sewing, I remember the thimble and the ring she had.
Of course, the wedding ring, and she had another ring next to it.
And when she was sewing, I remember the thimble knocking against the rings.
And that sound I still remember, just like I remember the smell of my father's paintbrushes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When he was taken away in 1940 to the camp, he left me in charge of his paintbrushes.
He told me how to soak them, how to clean them, so when he comes back, he could still use them.
And I remember the smell of the paint always in my nose.
Yes.
My mother was born, if I remember correctly-- they were talking with-- in 1897.
I don't remember the month.
I don't remember the birthday.
And my father was born in 1899.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my grandparents, both my grandparents, very vividly.
With my maternal grandparents, we lived the first year or the first few months in the ghetto.
Because they lived in Balut, which is the section of town that was cordoned off for the ghetto.
We lived in town.
So we moved in with them until we got our own apartment.
My-- their name was Morvitz.
Joseph Morvitz was my grandfather.
I don't remember my grandmother's first name.
He was a, a printer on cloths.
He printed materials.
He worked in a factory.
I remember they had, like a, like a big square with a pattern on the inside.
He used to paint over the pattern and print the material.
And in the winter, I remember his fingers across here were cracked, cracked up.
You could see the blood from the cold and from the wetness, from the paints, or I don't know, the chemicals.
My grandmother was a housewife.
I even remember my great-grandmother.
But I don't know whether she was the mother of my grandfather or my grandmother.
And about her, there was a standing joke in the family.
They took a family picture before she died with all the aunts and uncles and my grandparents.
And she looked at the picture.
And she said-- I hope it sounds the same in English.
She said it in Yiddish.
I look too mature.
She was, I think, 94 or 95 at the time.
When she died, they told me that she got dressed in the morning, and she wore those high buttoned-up shoes.
And it was all buttoned up.
She was completely dressed with a little diamond or a pearl she had here.
And she just died from old age, very quietly.
She-- I don't think she was sick or anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My paternal grandparents, my grandfather Heinich Levin and [NON-ENGLISH] Levin, my grandmother.
My grandfather had what you would call now a transportation company.
The transportation company consisted of a horse and buggy.
He used to cart materials and all kinds of stuff from the train station and deliver it into town.
And my biggest pleasure was to go with my grandfather.
My, my parents didn't like it.
But I used to love it, going with my grandfather when he took the horse to put it away in the stables for the night.
The biggest pleasure was if he put me on the horse.
And I was riding the horse.
Of course, he held on to me.
[SIGHS] I used to sleep over once in a while.
So I was with my cousins.
And that was a great pleasure, because another aunt, my father's oldest sister, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]-- I forgot her last name.
My god.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
Because I had two cousins.
They had a, a son and a daughter.
And their son, [NON-ENGLISH], he was the only one that was older than me in the whole family.
He was a year older than me.
He's gone, too.
I used to love sleeping over there with the aunts and the uncles, everybody.
And they lived in the same house.
My oldest aunt lived in the same building, so I was able to play with my cousins.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then it came 1939.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We-- in September, were the first few days, as soon as the war started, the Germans were in Lodz.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
No, my family was not religious.
My parents, my parents were the first generation that made the revolution.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1916, '17-- in fact, my father was in a concentration camp during First World War, in a German concentration camp.
And the stories he used to tell us about people freezing to death there, that's all I remember.
They were the first one to make the revolution to come out of religion.
And being the very first generation, the first people, they were very radical.
Well, now it would be [INAUDIBLE], but at that time was very radical.
My father took off his hat.
He didn't wear a hat.
That was it.
He remembered telling me that his father told him that if you come home with a hat, you know, a modern hat, I'm going to cut it up and throw it out.
So he told him, if you do that, you'll never see me again.
Apparently, he didn't cut his hat.
And they were very close, even though they didn't like the idea that he wasn't religious.
And I was brought up without religion completely.
The school I went to was non-religious.
And my mother also-- she was the only one-- well, they were-- she had a brother, two brothers and a sister.
And she was also the first one to break away from religion.
That's why they gave me this kind of an upbringing and this kind of an education.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had friends used to come in our house.
I remember my parents' friends getting together, singing new songs.
They had-- we had one friend, my parents' friend, that had a brother in the United States in New York.
He was a composer, very famous Yiddish composer, [NON-ENGLISH].
He worked for the [INAUDIBLE], all the modern Yiddish songs that are taught in schools.
So we had it first-hand.
And she was a great singer.
This friend, [NON-ENGLISH], her daughter went to school with me, [INAUDIBLE].
And we had all the new songs, the new modern songs.
So I always remember singing in our house when the friends got together.
We didn't have a big place-- one, one room.
Was one mid-sized room.
It was maybe a little bit bigger than this living room but not much.
So there was the bedroom, the kitchen, the dining room, the living room, everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It was a lot of fun.
I used to like that when we-- even though I had to go to sleep in the same room.
But I still listened to the songs.
And then we used to sing the songs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was fun.
There are so many instances.
But who can remember?
Who can bring it out of memory?
You didn't have any brothers or sisters?
I never had brothers or sisters.
Later on after the war I was thinking about that.
In one way, I was glad that I never had any because there would have been more victims.
And then again, I thought upside if they would have survived, I would have had somebody.
This way, I had nobody from my whole family.
The only family I have now is through Regina, through my wife, because she has a lot of cousins all over the world that survived.
From my family, nobody survived.
I looked around.
We went all over Germany after the war to look for people, didn't find anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I guess that's all I can remember of any significance from before the war, from my childhood.
I'm trying to think.
I, I can't remember anything that would be of particular interest from my childhood.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Always.
There were always arguments with my friends.
I remember they had a couple of people, a couple of friends, that were Communists.
And the fights were always-- they were always fighting about it.
Because of course, being a, the Bund socialist, we were very anti-communist.
But at that time, it was a different way.
We didn't know what was going on in Russia at that time.
Before the war, I'm talking about.
Before 1937, when we started hearing all the good things about the killing of all the, the pioneers of the revolution.
Yeah, there were constant discussions always.
That's all I remember, constant.
Even-- when I had a birthday, that's we-- every year, even though we weren't rich, my parents made a party for my birthday.
And of course, all the kids got together.
And then later on, the grown-ups came.
And there were always discussion, until the kids started rebelling.
This is our party.
No discussions.
But there were always, always political discussion.
They wanted to change the world.
They wanted to make a revolution, the fascist Poland.
And of course, there was plenty to talk about with the persecution and Poland against the Jews.
I never had any Polish friends.

It's impossible to believe now-- living in the United States 30 years and then 15 years in Australia before that-- to live in a country, which is predominantly Gentile, and not to have any Gentile kids friends.
I lived-- we lived in a building, there were maybe three or four Jewish families.
The rest were Gentiles.
They had kids.
I never had any Polish friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's impossible now.
It boggles the mind.
I can't understand how that would be possible.
And we weren't newcomers in Poland.
We had lived there 1,000 years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was, I think, a self-imposed segregation.
Oh, yes, constantly.
We were scared of the-- we walked down the street, used to call us [NON-ENGLISH], filthy Jew.
That-- all that was a constant.
That was a thing that was an everyday-- it was part of the life, our life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All the time.
There was not-- if I walked out in the street, you were really-- you were scared.
But we lived.
We managed to live.
Maybe that's why when we came in together when the war started, of course, there was a tremendous difference.
I mean, God forbid, you can't compare it.
But it was just like going from one stage to another.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the German Jews came to Poland, for them, it was a terrible comedown, because their standard of living was higher.
For us, it was like a gradual going from one persecution into another, into a more horrible one, of course.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yeah, that was, I think-- in fact, it was our family.
It was, I believe, my mother's cousins that came from Berlin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They weren't-- well, they couldn't live with us.
There was no room.
But we-- I remember them.
I was so envious of the kids, my cousins.
They had fountain pens, silver-colored, silver fountain pens.
You know, they were probably nickel or something.
I couldn't-- the way they were dressed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was like from a different planet.
Their standard of living was 1,000% higher than ours, in Germany.
But then, they were kicked out.
In-- they came through [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the famous little town on the Polish-German border, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they came to Lodz.
They lived a-- I think they came into the ghetto later on.
But this was the impression I had.
I was so envious of those people because of-- of course, the last-- I was what, at that time? 10 years old.
I didn't think about them losing their homes and being kicked out of-- after living for generations in Germany.
This is the impression I had.
My god, these people must be millionaires.
This had to be have been in '37 or '38 when they started seriously kicking all the Jews out of Germany.
They just threw them over the border of Poland.
That was it.
Well, the great change came with the war in 1939.
This is when the change came.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As soon as the war started-- how long could it last?
In Lodz, it lasted a few days.
Germans were in Lodz.
They just walked and marched into Lodz.
That was it, without a shot, practically.
Lodz wasn't bombed.
We just saw the planes going through to Warsaw.
After they took Lodz, they bombed the hell out of Warsaw.
I mean, it was completely flattened.
But Lodz, hardly anything.
I don't think there was one building ruined in Lodz.
They just walked in.
You mean before the war?
I don't remember that.
No, that I don't remember.
There probably were.
I'm sure there were, you know, from the newspapers, you know, hearing the news.
This is all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our only source of information.
When you're-- newspaper, we didn't have a radio.
At the house, we didn't have a telephone.
I remember the first-- the last few days before the war started, we went downstairs.
One floor below us a family lived that had a big store, a hardware store, [NON-ENGLISH].
They had a radio.
So we all went down to listen to the radio, the news.
And I remember my mother's reaction when she heard the Polish president blessing the army.
She said, well, this is it.
It's all over.
If he's blessing the army already, the war has started.
And sure enough-- was it September 1, 1939?
It's another day I'll never forget.
I remember I ran out to get the newspaper.
Usually I think we had it delivered.
We went to buy.
I went out to buy the Yiddish newspaper that we were getting.
See, we didn't even get a Polish newspaper in our house.
My parents never-- I don't think I ever saw a Polish newspaper in our house, only Yiddish newspaper.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And that's when everything started.
And I saw the huge letters, the headlines.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hitler started the war against Poland, without even, I think, declaring war.
They marched across the border.
And that was before England got in.
That's all I can remember, the, the huge headlines, all about that big.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when they started to discuss the plans about leaving.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The men left.
A lot of men left.
They went to Russia, made towards the Polish, to Polish-Russian border.
You know when the Germans occupied Poland, they divided it.
Stalin took half of Poland.
Hitler took the other half, at the Bug, at the River Bug.
And a lot of people left Lodz, walked, got the horse and buggies, train, whatever they could.
They went east.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father didn't go.
He stayed with us.
There were discussions.
I think they were talking about leaving.
He was talking about it.
I don't think it ever entered that the whole family should leave.
This discussion never came up that he should leave.
But he didn't.
Apparently, he didn't want to leave the family, didn't want to go.
He didn't feel it was fair for just save his life, or he didn't know whether he will be able to save his life.
Because a lot of people did.
A lot of people died.
A lot of people saved their life, that they went to Russia.
A lot of them finished up in Russian concentration camps.
And a lot of them survived, just like we survived.
So he stayed with us.
It was very tough living in-- and then-- and we had new laws came out every day practically for the Jews.
